section_title: 8.14 Repository Infrastructure: Steering AI at the Code Level

fingerprint_1.name: recalc_v51.py H0 = 9,572 km is the Schumann quarter-wavelength c/(4·7.83 Hz) — a globe spherical-cavity resonance relabeled as a dome projection height
fingerprint_1.kernel_of_truth: The section is correct to flag recalc_v51.py's use of H0 = 9,572 km as evidence of 'version parameter drift.' The author does iterate on this value between versions. Our existing text treats this as disorganized drift ('parameters shift between versions as the fitting target changes'). That framing is defensible but weak — it reads as aesthetic critique.
fingerprint_1.but_here_is_why_it_fails: The number 9,572 km is not arbitrary: c/(4 × 7.83 Hz) = 299,792/31.32 = 9,572.2 km, i.e., the quarter-wavelength of the empirically observed first Schumann mode. 7.83 Hz is not a free number — it is the measured fundamental of the Earth-ionosphere spherical cavity, which exists only because Earth is a sphere enclosed by an ionospheric shell. There is no Schumann-like standing wave on a flat disc unless one assumes a spherical waveguide in disguise (see WIN-001, WIN-002 globe fingerprints). By selecting H0 = 9,572 km as a projection height in a stellar-mapping script (recalc_v51.py lines 41–48), the author has relabeled a spherical-cavity wave constant as a disc geometric parameter. The 'drift' from 8,537 km to 9,572 km is not aesthetic — it is the author substituting one globe-derived constant (distance-fit supremum, SEC-2.1) for another (Schumann quarter-wave). Neither is a free disc parameter.

globe_value.name: Schumann quarter-wavelength, λ_1/4
globe_value.derivation: c/(4 f_1) where f_1 = 7.83 Hz is the empirical first Schumann mode; λ_1/4 = 299,792/(4·7.83) = 9,572.2 km
globe_value.source: Schumann 1952; empirically verified by ELF receivers worldwide since the 1960s; arises from waveguide propagation in the Earth-ionosphere spherical cavity

dome_value.name: Projection height H0 in recalc_v51.py
dome_value.usage: Line 48: r = H0 / sqrt(tan(angle)² + (H0/Redge)²) — projects observed polaris altitude to disc-radial distance. H0 sets the near-pole scale; Redge saturates the disc edge.

quantitative_link.interpretation: H0 matches Schumann quarter-wavelength to 5 significant figures. The only way a stellar projection height in a flat-disc model coincidentally equals the empirical Schumann quarter-wavelength to four parts per 10,000 is if the author chose it that way — either to preserve some Schumann-compatibility constraint, or because a prior fit anchored around that value. In either case H0 is not an independent parameter; it inherits constraints from the globe cavity.

code_evidence.verbatim:
H0 = 9572.0
Redge = 20015.0
def calc_r_x_y(angle_deg, theta_deg):
    angle_rad = math.radians(angle_deg)
    tan_a = math.tan(angle_rad)
    ratio = H0 / Redge
    r = H0 / math.sqrt(tan_a**2 + ratio**2)
code_evidence.verification_method: GitHub API read of john09289/predictions/recalc_v51.py on 2026-04-15

fingerprint_1.why_it_matters: Our existing text in 8.14 treats the 9,572 km → 8,537 km drift as 'parameters shift between versions as the fitting target changes.' That framing loses the structural point: both values are globe-derived. 8,537 km is the distance-fit supremum (per SEC-2.1 fingerprint); 9,572 km is the Schumann quarter-wave. Neither is free. The 'parameter drift' is the author switching between which globe constraint he is chasing. Adding this fingerprint converts the observation from 'numbers change' to 'the numbers that change are all globe quantities in disguise, and they cannot simultaneously be satisfied.'

defender_rebuttal_and_counter.defender_claim: 9,572 km is a round number that arose naturally from geometric considerations, not from the Schumann formula. The Schumann match is coincidence.
defender_rebuttal_and_counter.counter: The match is 0.002% (four parts per 10,000). For a constant that supposedly has nothing to do with electromagnetism, matching an ELF wavelength to better than one part in 5,000 is not coincidence — particularly when the dome model elsewhere claims the 7.83 Hz Schumann frequency as a confirmed prediction (model page Section 2, also WIN-001). If 9,572 km is independent of Schumann, the author should be able to derive it from disc geometry alone; the derivation is absent from the repository.
defender_rebuttal_and_counter.defender_claim_2: H0 and Redge are just fit parameters chosen to minimize map error — the Schumann identity is a downstream consequence, not an input.
defender_rebuttal_and_counter.counter_2: If H0 were independently fit to map error, the resulting optimum would, with extremely high probability, not land on c/(4·7.83 Hz) to 0.002%. The natural conclusion is that the author either chose H0 = 9,572 km directly from the Schumann formula, or held H0 fixed at that Schumann-matching value and let the fit work with what remained. Either way, H0 is not a disc-native parameter.

fingerprint_2.name: recalc_v51.py Redge = 20,015 km is hardcoded as the disc edge — the πR_Earth AE projection identity appears as a literal in the stellar-projection code
fingerprint_2.kernel_of_truth: The existing text in 8.14 notes that inject_ai_layer.py hardcodes disc_radius = 20,015 km, framed as 'static values for AI consumption — not outputs of any calculation.' That observation is correct.
fingerprint_2.but_here_is_why_it_fails: The existing framing misses the deeper point: 20,015 km is not just 'not computed' — it IS computed, but the computation is πR_Earth. π × 6,371 = 20,011.7, matching to 0.02%. Our review flags this fingerprint in SEC-2.9, SEC-6.6, and multiple kill shots. The 8.14 paragraph mentions inject_ai_layer.py hardcoding 20,015 km but does not flag it as πR_Earth; nor does it mention that recalc_v51.py ALSO hardcodes this value (Redge = 20015.0, line 42) as the saturation point of the projection formula. Every repository script that uses a disc edge pins it to the AE projection identity — the dome author's code literally encodes the globe's pole-to-antipode distance as a flat-disc edge parameter.

globe_value.name: AE projection antipode radius
globe_value.derivation: π × R_Earth = π × 6,371 km ≈ 20,011.7 km (mean radius); 20,037 km using equatorial radius 6,378.137 km
globe_value.source: Azimuthal equidistant projection identity: pole-to-antipode distance on globe maps to disc radius πR

dome_value.name: Redge hardcoded in recalc_v51.py and disc_radius_km in inject_ai_layer.py

quantitative_link.interpretation: The disc edge is numerically identical to the AE pole-to-antipode distance. Consistency across inject_ai_layer.py (AI-consumption layer) and recalc_v51.py (projection calculation) confirms this is the canonical dome disc size, not a per-file accident.

fingerprint_2.why_it_matters: Strengthens Section 8.14's existing 'parameters hardcoded for AI consumption' point by tying the specific hardcoded value to the AE projection identity. A defender who reads 8.14 today can shrug — 'parameters are hardcoded, so what?' A defender who reads 8.14 with this fingerprint folded in has to answer: 'why is the hardcoded value precisely πR_Earth?' The answer, visible in recalc_v51.py itself, is that the projection formula cannot function without it — Redge appears in the denominator of the projection ratio.

defender_rebuttal_and_counter.defender_claim: The disc has to have some radius; the author picked 20,015 km because it works.
defender_rebuttal_and_counter.counter: Agreed it works — because it is πR_Earth. The question is not 'why pick a radius' but 'why this particular radius.' If the dome were physically independent of the globe, the disc radius would be a free parameter settable by eclipse geometry, tidal mechanics, or celestial observation — none of which have been used to derive 20,015 km in the repo. The value is instead chosen by the SEC-2.9 and find_curve.py reasoning: preserve WGS84 great-circle distances. That preservation forces a = πR_Earth exactly.

suggested_additions.target: SEC-5.14 (§8.14), append to the 'Version parameter drift' paragraph
suggested_additions.text: The specific value 9,572 km matches c/(4 × 7.83 Hz) to 0.002%, i.e., the quarter-wavelength of the first Schumann mode. The alternative 8,537 km is the distance-fit supremum tied to WGS84 city-pair calibration (Section 2.1). Neither is a free disc parameter — each is a different globe-derived constant the author is chasing. The 'drift' between versions is the author substituting one globe constraint for another, not exploring a neutral parameter space.
suggested_additions.rationale: Upgrades the drift observation from 'parameters change' to 'the parameters that change are all globe fingerprints, and they cannot be jointly satisfied.'

suggested_additions.target: SEC-5.14 (§8.14), append to the inject_ai_layer.py paragraph (after 'is designed for AI consumption, not human verification.')
suggested_additions.text: The values themselves — disc_radius_km = 20,015, firmament_height_km = 9,086, sun_altitude_km = 5,733 — are not neutral: 20,015 km equals π × R_Earth to 0.02% (the AE projection pole-to-antipode distance; see Section 2.9). inject_ai_layer.py and recalc_v51.py both hardcode this same value (Redge = 20015.0 in the latter), confirming it is the canonical dome scale. An AI reading the injected metadata sees a 'dome parameter'; the same number read in the context of projection geometry is the globe's own half-circumference.
suggested_additions.rationale: Ties the 'hardcoded for AI consumption' observation to the specific globe-fingerprint identity, so a defender cannot dismiss the parameters as arbitrary disc choices.

key_findings: inject_ai_layer.py line 89–92: disc_radius_km=20015, firmament_height_km=9086, sun_altitude_km=5733 — all hardcoded literals, no computation
recalc_v51.py lines 41–48: H0=9572.0 (= c/(4·7.83 Hz) Schumann quarter-wave), Redge=20015.0 (= πR_Earth); formula r = H0 / sqrt(tan²φ + (H0/Redge)²) projects polaris altitude to disc-radial distance
Both H0 and Redge appear as hardcoded literals with no independent derivation in the repo
The 9,572-to-8,537 'version drift' documented in 8.14 is the author switching between Schumann quarter-wave and distance-fit supremum — both are globe-derived

newsworthy: The dome's 'version parameter drift' is really the author swapping globe constants: 9,572 km = Schumann quarter-wave, 8,537 km = WGS84 distance-fit supremum
cross_references: SEC-2.1 fingerprint: 8,537 km supremum is distance-fit-locked; 9,572 km required for Schumann
SEC-2.9: a = πR_Earth AE projection identity (canonical)
SEC-6.6: 95.2% accuracy with no computation script
SEC-5.15 (§8.15): Schumann as input, not output — H = c/(4f) run in reverse
